title: The Youth Of Nature
content:
آ آ آ آ Raisï؟½d are the dripping oars
آ آ آ آ Silent the boat: the lake,
آ آ آ آ Lovely and soft as a dream,
آ آ آ آ Swims in the sheen of the moon.
آ آ آ آ The mountains stand at its head
آ آ آ آ Clear in the pure June night,
آ آ آ آ But the valleys are flooded with haze.
آ آ آ آ Rydal and Fairfield are there;
آ آ آ آ In the shadow Wordsworth lies dead.
آ آ آ آ So it is, so it will be for aye.
آ آ آ آ Nature is fresh as of old,
آ آ آ آ Is lovely: a mortal is dead.

آ آ آ آ The spots which recall him survive,
آ آ آ آ For he lent a new life to these hills.
آ آ آ آ The Pillar still broods oï؟½er the fields
آ آ آ آ Which border Ennerdale Lake,
آ آ آ آ And Egremont sleeps by the sea.
آ آ آ آ The gleam of The Evening Star
آ آ آ آ Twinkles on Grasmere no more,
آ آ آ آ But ruinï؟½d and solemn and grey
آ آ آ آ The sheepfold of Michael survives,
آ آ آ آ And far to the south, the heath
آ آ آ آ Still blows in the Quantock coombs,
آ آ آ آ By the favourite waters of Ruth.
آ آ آ آ These survive: yet not without pain,
آ آ آ آ Pain and dejection to-night,
آ آ آ آ Can I feel that their Poet is gone.

آ آ آ آ He grew old in an age he condemnï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ He lookï؟½d on the rushing decay
آ آ آ آ Of the times which had shelterï؟½d his youth.
آ آ آ آ Felt the dissolving throes
آ آ آ آ Of a social order he lovï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Outlivï؟½d his brethren, his peers.
آ آ آ آ And, like the Theban seer,
آ آ آ آ Died in his enemiesï؟½ day.

آ آ آ آ Cold bubbled the spring of Tilphusa,
آ آ آ آ Copais lay bright in the moon;
آ آ آ آ Helicon glassï؟½d in the lake
آ آ آ آ Its firs, and afar, rose the peaks
آ آ آ آ Of Parnassus, snowily clear:
آ آ آ آ Thebes was behind him in flames,
آ آ آ آ And the clang of arms in his ear,
آ آ آ آ When his awe-struck captors led
آ آ آ آ The Theban seer to the spring.
آ آ آ آ Tiresias drank and died.
آ آ آ آ Nor did reviving Thebes
آ آ آ آ See such a prophet again.

آ آ آ آ Well may we mourn, when the head
آ آ آ آ Of a sacred poet lies low
آ آ آ آ In an age which can rear them no more.
آ آ آ آ The complaining millions of men
آ آ آ آ Darken in labour and pain;
آ آ آ آ But he was a priest to us all
آ آ آ آ Of the wonder and bloom of the world,
آ آ آ آ Which we saw with his eyes, and were glad.
آ آ آ آ He is dead, and the fruit-bearing day
آ آ آ آ Of his race is past on the earth;
آ آ آ آ And darkness returns to our eyes.

آ آ آ آ For oh, is it you, is it you,
آ آ آ آ Moonlight, and shadow, and lake,
آ آ آ آ And mountains, that fill us with joy,
آ آ آ آ Or the Poet who sings you so well
آ آ آ آ Is it you, O Beauty, O Grace,
آ آ آ آ O Charm, O Romance, that we feel,
آ آ آ آ Or the voice which reveals what you are?
آ آ آ آ Are ye, like daylight and sun,
آ آ آ آ Sharï؟½d and rejoicï؟½d in by all?
آ آ آ آ Or are ye immersï؟½d in the mass
آ آ آ آ Of matter, and hard to extract,
آ آ آ آ Or sunk at the core of the world
آ آ آ آ Too deep for the most to discern
آ آ آ آ Like stars in the deep of the sky,
آ آ آ آ Which arise on the glass of the sage,
آ آ آ آ But are lost when their watcher is gone.

آ آ آ آ They are hereï؟½ I heard, as men heard
آ آ آ آ In Mysian Ida the voice
آ آ آ آ Of the Mighty Mother, or Crete,
آ آ آ آ The murmur of Nature reply
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Loveliness, Magic, and Grace,
آ آ آ آ They are here they are set in the world
آ آ آ آ They abide and the finest of souls
آ آ آ آ Has not been thrillï؟½d by them all,
آ آ آ آ Nor the dullest been dead to them quite.
آ آ آ آ The poet who sings them may die,
آ آ آ آ But they are immortal, and live,
آ آ آ آ For they are the life of the world.
آ آ آ آ Will ye not learn it, and know,
آ آ آ آ When ye mourn that a poet is dead,
آ آ آ آ That the singer was less than his themes,
آ آ آ آ Life, and Emotion, and I?

آ آ آ آ ï؟½More than the singer are these.
آ آ آ آ Weak is the tremor of pain
آ آ آ آ That thrills in his mournfullest chord
آ آ آ آ To that which once ran through his soul.
آ آ آ آ Cold the elation of joy
آ آ آ آ In his gladdest, airiest song,
آ آ آ آ To that which of old in his youth
آ آ آ آ Fillï؟½d him and made him divine.
آ آ آ آ Hardly his voice at its best
آ آ آ آ Gives us a sense of the awe,
آ آ آ آ The vastness, the grandeur, the gloom
آ آ آ آ Of the unlit gulph of himself.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Ye know not yourselves and your bards,
آ آ آ آ The clearest, the best, who have read
آ آ آ آ Most in themselves, have beheld
آ آ آ آ Less than they left unrevealï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Ye express not yourselves can ye make
آ آ آ آ With marble, with colour, with word,
آ آ آ آ What charmï؟½d you in others re-live?
آ آ آ آ Can thy pencil, O Artist, restore
آ آ آ آ The figure, the bloom of thy love,
آ آ آ آ As she was in her morning of spring?
آ آ آ آ Canst thou paint the ineffable smile
آ آ آ آ Of her eyes as they rested on thine?
آ آ آ آ Can the image of life have the glow,
آ آ آ آ The motion of life itself

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Yourselves and your fellows ye know not and me
آ آ آ آ The Mateless, the One, will ye know?
آ آ آ آ Will ye scan me, and read me, and tell
آ آ آ آ Of the thoughts that ferment in my breast,
آ آ آ آ My longing, my sadness, my joy?
آ آ آ آ Will ye claim for your great ones the gift
آ آ آ آ To have renderï؟½d the gleam of my skies,
آ آ آ آ To have echoed the moan of my seas,
آ آ آ آ Utterï؟½d the voice of my hills?
آ آ آ آ When your great ones depart, will ye say
آ آ آ آ All things have sufferï؟½d a loss
آ آ آ آ Nature is hid in their grave?

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Race after race, man after man,
آ آ آ آ Have dreamï؟½d that my secret was theirs,
آ آ آ آ Have thought that I livï؟½d but for them,
آ آ آ آ That they were my glory and joy.
آ آ آ آ They are dust, they are changï؟½d, they are gone.
آ آ آ آ I remain.ï؟½
meaning:
This evocative piece by Matthew Arnold represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Matthew Arnold's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
